label: Cautiously hopeful
summary: The G7 summit at Évian closed on its third day without a joint communiqué for the second straight year, leaving the week's defining diplomacy half-finished rather than sealed. The U.S.-Iran accord to end their three-month war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz still pointed toward a formal signing Friday, June 19 at Switzerland's Bürgenstock resort - Vice President Vance called the memorandum 'about 1.5 pages' framing 60 days of nuclear talks - and a sanctioned Iranian tanker crossed the U.S. naval blockade in the Gulf of Oman as the cordon began lifting. Brent crude sat near $80 a barrel. But the deal is not yet ink: Israel, excluded from it, kept striking southern Lebanon, killing at least four more on June 16, and President Trump openly chided Prime Minister Netanyahu for not finishing the 'minor war' faster while Tehran warned any continued Israeli presence in Lebanon would breach the accord. On the markets, the Federal Reserve held its target range at 3.50-3.75% in new Chair Kevin Warsh's first decision, with May inflation still elevated at 4.2%; the Nasdaq had slipped the day before even as the Dow held near records. Ukraine offered fragile hope - a Zelensky-Trump bilateral on the summit's margins and Russian signals of fresh talks after a June 22 prisoner exchange - against no ceasefire. The harder ledger held: Sudan's IPC-confirmed famine in El Fasher and Kadugli ground on with 21.2 million people, 45% of the country, acutely food-insecure and aid convoys still blocked or attacked. Set against that, science delivered - a complete brain-to-body wiring map of the fruit fly, sharper virus imaging via nanodiscs - Pacific nations drew their first climate-insurance payouts in Tuvalu, and clean energy kept gaining. A war edging toward signature but not signed, a steady-handed Fed and live science keep the mood at the soft middle of good, pulled no higher by the missing communiqué, Lebanon's strikes and Sudan's famine.

drivers.headline: The U.S.-Iran accord to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz heads toward a formal signing Friday, June 19 at Switzerland's Burgenstock resort; VP Vance calls the memorandum 'about 1.5 pages' framing 60 days of nuclear talks, and a sanctioned Iranian tanker crosses the lifting U.S. naval blockade in the Gulf of Oman

G7 leaders close the Evian summit on its final day without a joint communique for the second consecutive year, as Trump's tariff threats - including 100% duties on French wine over its digital tax - and splits over rare-earth supply leave the alliance divided

The Federal Reserve holds its target range at 3.50-3.75% in new Chair Kevin Warsh's first decision, with markets having priced a ~97% chance of a hold; the updated dot plot and Warsh's debut press conference draw the focus as May inflation runs elevated at 4.2%

Brent crude sits near $80 a barrel - down from above $100 earlier in the crisis - as the U.S.-Iran deal points to a reopened Strait of Hormuz and recovering Gulf oil flows once the accord takes effect

Israel, excluded from the Iran deal, keeps striking southern Lebanon - killing at least four near Nabatieh on June 16 - as Trump chides Netanyahu for not finishing the 'minor war' faster and Tehran warns any continued Israeli presence in Lebanon would breach the accord

Ukraine diplomacy flickers at Evian: a Zelensky-Trump bilateral on the summit's margins and Russian signals of a fresh negotiating round after a June 22 prisoner-and-remains exchange, even as no ceasefire is reached and the G7 weighs further costs on Moscow

Sudan's famine grinds on: with IPC-confirmed famine in besieged El Fasher and Kadugli, WFP, FAO and UNICEF report about 21.2 million people - 45% of the population - acutely food-insecure, malnutrition screening of 38-75% in El Fasher, and El Fasher unreachable by road for over a year

Trump and India's Modi hold a closely watched bilateral on the G7's final day, with trade, tariffs and tech alliances topping the agenda as Washington seeks to align partners ahead of looming tariff deadlines
Scientists complete the first full brain-to-body wiring map of the fruit fly, a landmark connectomics dataset linking neural circuits to the muscles they control

Pacific resilience advances as Tuvalu pays out its first climate-insurance claims - AU$75 each to over 400 households hit by March high tides - while Australia and New Zealand pledge deeper energy-security and defence cooperation with island nations
Argentina's country risk falls to its lowest level since April 2018, President Milei says, an investor vote of confidence even as Colombia heads into a tense June 21 presidential runoff

Good-news ledger: Europe reconnected over 2,300 miles of rivers as a record 603 dam barriers came down last year, a new 200,000-square-mile marine reserve was declared around French Polynesia's Austral and Marquesas islands, and U.S. solar out-generated coal for a first full month

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (52nd G7 summit / 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis)
